title: Quantum Measurement

theory.introduction: Measurement is the process of extracting classical information from a quantum system. When we measure a qubit, it stops behaving like a quantum object and gives a definite classical result (0 or 1).
theory.start_from_zero: Before measurement, a qubit exists in a superposition of states. This means it does not have a definite value. Measurement forces the qubit to 'choose' one outcome based on probabilities.
theory.why_it_matters: Measurement is how we get useful results from quantum computers. All quantum computations eventually end with measurement, which converts quantum information into classical data we can read.
theory.core_idea: A qubit stores probabilities in its amplitudes. Measurement converts those probabilities into a single definite outcome.
key_points: Measurement gives a definite classical outcome (0 or 1)
The quantum state collapses after measurement
Measurement depends on the chosen basis
Measurement does not reveal amplitudes directly, only outcomes
step_by_step_understanding: Step 1: Prepare a qubit (e.g., apply H)
Step 2: The qubit is in superposition
Step 4: A random outcome is produced based on probabilities
Step 5: The qubit collapses to that outcome

math.explanation: The square of the amplitude gives the probability of measuring that state.

intuition.coin: Like stopping a spinning coin — you force the system to choose heads or tails.
intuition.lottery: You can think of amplitudes as 'weights' in a lottery. Measurement picks one outcome based on those weights.
intuition.not_random_guess: The result may look random, but it follows precise probability rules.

sections.title: Measurement in Computational Basis
content: Standard measurement is done in the |0⟩, |1⟩ basis
Probability of 0 = |α|²
Probability of 1 = |β|²

sections.title: State Collapse
content: After measurement, the qubit collapses to the observed state
If result is 0 → state becomes |0⟩
If result is 1 → state becomes |1⟩
The original superposition is destroyed
sections.important_note: Collapse is not gradual — it happens instantly when measurement occurs.

sections.title: Measurement is Probabilistic, Not Arbitrary
content: Measurement outcomes appear random
But probabilities are strictly determined by amplitudes
Running the same circuit many times reveals the probability distribution

sections.title: Measurement Operators (Intro)
content: Measurement can be described using operators
Projector for |0⟩: P₀ = |0⟩⟨0|
Projector for |1⟩: P₁ = |1⟩⟨1|
Probability = ⟨ψ|P|ψ⟩

sections.title: Measurement in Different Bases
content: Measurement depends on the basis you choose
Default is computational basis (Z-basis)
We can change basis using gates
examples: Apply H before measurement → changes basis
sections.intuition: Changing basis is like rotating the coordinate system before measuring.

sections.title: Bloch Sphere Interpretation
content: Measurement corresponds to projecting the qubit onto an axis
Z-basis measurement → projection onto vertical axis
X-basis measurement → projection onto horizontal axis
The closer the state is to a pole, the higher the probability of that outcome

sections.title: Irreversibility
content: Measurement destroys quantum information
You cannot recover the original superposition
This is why quantum algorithms delay measurement until the end

sections.title: Multiple Measurements
content: Measuring the same qubit again gives the same result
Because the state is already collapsed
Repeated measurements do not change the outcome

sections.title: Important Subtle Point (Phase)
content: Measurement depends only on |α|² and |β|²
Global phase does not affect measurement
Relative phase affects interference in circuits, not direct measurement

examples.title: Measure Superposition
examples.description: Apply Hadamard and measure the qubit.

examples.title: Repeated Measurement
examples.result: Second measurement gives same result as first

examples.title: Measurement in X-Basis
examples.description: Apply H before measurement to change basis.

common_mistakes: Thinking measurement reveals the full quantum state
Confusing randomness with lack of rules
Ignoring the role of basis in measurement
Assuming phase affects direct measurement outcome

try_this: Run the circuit multiple times and observe distribution
Apply Z before measurement and compare results
Apply H twice and observe deterministic outcome

quiz.question: What does measurement do to a quantum state?
What happens if you measure twice?
Does phase affect direct measurement outcome?
quiz.explanation: Only amplitude magnitudes affect measurement probabilities.
